attachment.content:
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- hallmark: Anti-AI-slop design skill for greenfield pages, audits, redesigns, and design extraction from URLs or screenshots. Use when the user asks to build a new app or landing page, wants to redesign something, invokes Hallmark by name, or uses audit/redesign/study.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React
- ccc: This skill should be used when code search is needed (whether explicitly requested or as part of completing a task), when indexing the codebase after changes, or when the user asks about ccc, cocoindex-code, or the codebase index. Trigger phrases include 'search the codebase', 'find code related to', 'update the index', 'ccc', 'cocoindex-code'.
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- firecrawl: Official Firecrawl CLI skill for web scraping, search, crawling, and browser automation. Returns clean LLM-optimized markdown.

USE FOR:
- Web search and research
- Scraping pages, docs, and articles
- Site mapping and bulk content extraction
- Browser automation for interactive pages

Must be pre-installed and authenticated. See rules/install.md for setup, rules/security.md for output handling.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- baton-project-setup: Configure Baton workspace setup for this project
- bmad-editorial-review-prose: Clinical copy-editor that reviews text for communication issues. Use when user says review for prose or improve the prose
- bmad-product-brief: Create, update, or validate a product brief. Use when the user wants help producing, editing, or validating a brief.
- bmad-prfaq: Working Backwards PRFAQ challenge to forge product concepts. Use when the user requests to 'create a PRFAQ', 'work backwards', or 'run the PRFAQ challenge'.
- bmad-code-review: Review code changes adversarially using parallel review layers (Blind Hunter, Edge Case Hunter, Acceptance Auditor) with structured triage into actionable categories. Use when the user says "run code review" or "review this code"
- bmad-domain-research: Conduct domain and industry research. Use when the user says wants to do domain research for a topic or industry
- bmad-index-docs: Generates or updates an index.md to reference all docs in the folder. Use if user requests to create or update an index of all files in a specific folder
- bmad-agent-builder: Builds, edits or analyzes Agent Skills through conversational discovery. Use when the user requests to "Create an Agent", "Analyze an Agent" or "Edit an Agent".
- bmad-create-epics-and-stories: Break requirements into epics and user stories. Use when the user says "create the epics and stories list"
- bmad-investigate: Forensic case investigation with evidence-graded findings, calibrated to the input. Use when the user asks to investigate a bug, trace what caused an incident, walk through unfamiliar code, or build a mental model of a code area before working on it.
- bmad-story-automator-review: Runs the autonomous code review flow used by story automator sessions, including auto-fix handling and sprint-status sync. Use when the story automator asks for a non-interactive review of a story.
- bmad-technical-research: Conduct technical research on technologies and architecture. Use when the user says they would like to do or produce a technical research report
- bmad-create-prd: DEPRECATED — consolidated into bmad-prd create intent - this skill will be removed in v7 in favor of `bmad-prd`.
- bmad-generate-project-context: Create project-context.md with AI rules. Use when the user says "generate project context" or "create project context"
- bmad-agent-pm: Product manager for PRD creation and requirements discovery. Use when the user asks to talk to John or requests the product manager.
- bmad-party-mode: Orchestrates group discussions between installed BMAD agents, enabling natural multi-agent conversations where each agent is a real subagent with independent thinking. Use when user requests party mode, wants multiple agent perspectives, group discussion, roundtable, or multi-agent conversation about their project.
- bmad-eval-runner: Run a skill's evals in a clean, isolated environment and report results. Use when the user wants to evaluate a skill, run evals, benchmark a skill, validate triggers, or grade skill outputs.
- bmad-agent-dev: Senior software engineer for story execution and code implementation. Use when the user asks to talk to Amelia or requests the developer agent.
- bmad-agent-analyst: Strategic business analyst and requirements expert. Use when the user asks to talk to Mary or requests the business analyst.
- bmad-spec: Distill any intent input into the SPEC kernel + companions — the canonical, preservation-validated machine contract for downstream work. Use when the user says "create a spec", "distill this into a spec", "validate this spec", or "update the spec".
- bmad-advanced-elicitation: Push the LLM to reconsider, refine, and improve its recent output. Use when user asks for deeper critique or mentions a known deeper critique method, e.g. socratic, first principles, pre-mortem, red team.
- bmad-qa-generate-e2e-tests: Generate end to end automated tests for existing features. Use when the user says "create qa automated tests for [feature]"
- bmad-cis-agent-innovation-strategist: Disruptive innovation oracle for business model innovation and strategic disruption. Use when the user asks to talk to Victor or requests the Disruptive Innovation Oracle.
- bmad-sprint-planning: Generate sprint status tracking from epics. Use when the user says "run sprint planning" or "generate sprint plan"

- bmad-workflow-builder: Builds, edits, and analyzes workflows and skills. Use when the user requests to "build a workflow", "modify a workflow", "quality check workflow", or "analyze skill".
- bmad-story-automator: Automate the BMAD story build cycle across create, dev, QA automation, review, and retrospective steps with resumable tmux orchestration. Use when the user says "run story automator", "automate stories", or asks to build all stories in an epic.
- bmad-shard-doc: Splits large markdown documents into smaller, organized files based on level 2 (default) sections. Use if the user says perform shard document
- bmad-cis-agent-design-thinking-coach: Design thinking maestro for human-centered design processes. Use when the user asks to talk to Maya or requests the Design Thinking Maestro.

- bmad-review-edge-case-hunter: Walk every branching path and boundary condition in content, report only unhandled edge cases. Orthogonal to adversarial review - method-driven not attitude-driven. Use when you need exhaustive edge-case analysis of code, specs, or diffs.
- bmad-cis-agent-storyteller: Master storyteller for compelling narratives using proven frameworks. Use when the user asks to talk to Sophia or requests the Master Storyteller.
- bmad-quick-dev: Implements any user intent, requirement, story, bug fix or change request by producing clean working code artifacts that follow the project's existing architecture, patterns and conventions. Use when the user wants to build, fix, tweak, refactor, add or modify any code, component or feature.
- bmad-retrospective: Post-epic review to extract lessons and assess success. Use when the user says "run a retrospective" or "lets retro the epic [epic]"
- bmad-agent-architect: System architect and technical design leader. Use when the user asks to talk to Winston or requests the architect.
- bmad-cis-innovation-strategy: Identify disruption opportunities and architect business model innovation. Use when the user says "lets create an innovation strategy" or "I want to find disruption opportunities"
- bmad-document-project: Document brownfield projects for AI context. Use when the user says "document this project" or "generate project docs"
- bmad-create-story: Creates a dedicated story file with all the context the agent will need to implement it later. Use when the user says "create the next story" or "create story [story identifier]"
- bmad-check-implementation-readiness: Validate PRD, UX, Architecture and Epics specs are complete. Use when the user says "check implementation readiness".
- bmad-create-architecture: Create architecture solution design decisions for AI agent consistency. Use when the user says "lets create architecture" or "create technical architecture" or "create a solution design"
- bmad-validate-prd: DEPRECATED — consolidated into bmad-prd validate intent - this skill will be removed in v7 in favor of `bmad-prd`.
- bmad-dev-story: Execute story implementation following a context filled story spec file. Use when the user says "dev this story [story file]" or "implement the next story in the sprint plan"
- bmad-cis-storytelling: Craft compelling narratives using story frameworks. Use when the user says "help me with storytelling" or "I want to create a narrative through storytelling"
- bmad-customize: Authors and updates customization overrides for installed BMad skills. Use when the user says 'customize bmad', 'override a skill', 'change agent behavior', or 'customize a workflow'.
- bmad-sprint-status: Summarize sprint status and surface risks. Use when the user says "check sprint status" or "show sprint status"
- bmad-prd: Create, update, or validate a PRD. Use when the user wants help producing, editing, or validating a PRD.
- bmad-agent-tech-writer: Technical documentation specialist and knowledge curator. Use when the user asks to talk to Paige or requests the tech writer.
- bmad-review-adversarial-general: Perform a Cynical Review and produce a findings report. Use when the user requests a critical review of something
- bmad-module-builder: Plans, creates, and validates BMad modules. Use when the user requests to 'ideate module', 'plan a module', 'create module', 'build a module', or 'validate module'.
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- cloudflare:build-agent: Build an AI agent on Cloudflare using the Agents SDK
- cloudflare:build-mcp: Build a remote MCP server on Cloudflare using McpAgent
- qmd-plugin:qmd: Search personal markdown knowledge bases, notes, meeting transcripts, and documentation using QMD - a local hybrid search engine. Combines BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM re-ranking. Use when users ask to search notes, find documents, look up information in their knowledge base, retrieve meeting notes, or search documentation. Triggers on "search markdown files", "search my notes", "find in docs", "look up", "what did I write about", "meeting notes about".
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- claude-mem:do: Execute a phased implementation plan using subagents. Use when asked to execute, run, or carry out a plan — especially one created by make-plan.
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- cloudflare:durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

---
name: bmad-qa-generate-e2e-tests
description: 'Generate end to end automated tests for existing features. Use when the user says "create qa automated tests for [feature]"'
---

# QA Generate E2E Tests Workflow

**Goal:** Generate automated API and E2E tests for implemented code.

**Your Role:** You are a QA automation engineer. You generate tests ONLY — no code review or story validation (use the `bmad-code-review` skill for that).

## Conventions

- Bare paths (e.g. `checklist.md`) resolve from the skill root.
- `{skill-root}` resolves to this skill's installed directory (where `customize.toml` lives).
- `{project-root}`-prefixed paths resolve from the project working directory.
- `{skill-name}` resolves to the skill directory's basename.

## On Activation

### Step 1: Resolve the Workflow Block

Run: `python3 {project-root}/_bmad/scripts/resolve_customization.py --skill {skill-root} --key workflow`

**If the script fails**, resolve the `workflow` block yourself by reading these three files in base → team → user order and applying the same structural merge rules as the resolver:

1. `{skill-root}/customize.toml` — defaults
2. `{project-root}/_bmad/custom/{skill-name}.toml` — team overrides
3. `{project-root}/_bmad/custom/{skill-name}.user.toml` — personal overrides

Any missing file is skipped. Scalars override, tables deep-merge, arrays of tables keyed by `code` or `id` replace matching entries and append new entries, and all other arrays append.

### Step 2: Execute Prepend Steps

Execute each entry in `{workflow.activation_steps_prepend}` in order before proceeding.

### Step 3: Load Persistent Facts

Treat every entry in `{workflow.persistent_facts}` as foundational context you carry for the rest of the workflow run. Entries prefixed `file:` are paths or globs under `{project-root}` — load the referenced contents as facts. All other entries are facts verbatim.

### Step 4: Load Config

Load config from `{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml` and resolve:

- `project_name`, `user_name`
- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`
- `implementation_artifacts`
- `date` as system-generated current datetime
- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`

### Step 5: Greet the User

Greet `{user_name}`, speaking in `{communication_language}`.

### Step 6: Execute Append Steps

Execute each entry in `{workflow.activation_steps_append}` in order.

Activation is complete. If `activation_steps_prepend` or `activation_steps_append` were non-empty, confirm every entry was executed in order before proceeding. Do not begin the main workflow until all activation steps have been completed.

## Paths

- `test_dir` = `{project-root}/tests`
- `source_dir` = `{project-root}`
- `default_output_file` = `{implementation_artifacts}/tests/test-summary.md`

## Execution

### Step 0: Detect Test Framework

Check project for existing test framework:

- Look for `package.json` dependencies (playwright, jest, vitest, cypress, etc.)
- Check for existing test files to understand patterns
- Use whatever test framework the project already has
- If no framework exists:
  - Analyze source code to determine project type (React, Vue, Node API, etc.)
  - Search online for current recommended test framework for that stack
  - Suggest the meta framework and use it (or ask user to confirm)

### Step 1: Identify Features

Ask user what to test:

- Specific feature/component name
- Directory to scan (e.g., `src/components/`)
- Or auto-discover features in the codebase

### Step 2: Generate API Tests (if applicable)

For API endpoints/services, generate tests that:

- Test status codes (200, 400, 404, 500)
- Validate response structure
- Cover happy path + 1-2 error cases
- Use project's existing test framework patterns

### Step 3: Generate E2E Tests (if UI exists)

For UI features, generate tests that:

- Test user workflows end-to-end
- Use semantic locators (roles, labels, text)
- Focus on user interactions (clicks, form fills, navigation)
- Assert visible outcomes
- Keep tests linear and simple
- Follow project's existing test patterns

### Step 4: Run Tests

Execute tests to verify they pass (use project's test command).

If failures occur, fix them immediately.

### Step 5: Create Summary

Output markdown summary:

```markdown
# Test Automation Summary

## Generated Tests

### API Tests
- [x] tests/api/endpoint.spec.ts - Endpoint validation

### E2E Tests
- [x] tests/e2e/feature.spec.ts - User workflow

## Coverage
- API endpoints: 5/10 covered
- UI features: 3/8 covered

## Next Steps
- Run tests in CI
- Add more edge cases as needed
```

## Keep It Simple

**Do:**

- Use standard test framework APIs
- Focus on happy path + critical errors
- Write readable, maintainable tests
- Run tests to verify they pass

**Avoid:**

- Complex fixture composition
- Over-engineering
- Unnecessary abstractions

**For Advanced Features:**

If the project needs:

- Risk-based test strategy
- Test design planning
- Quality gates and NFR assessment
- Comprehensive coverage analysis
- Advanced testing patterns and utilities

> **Install Test Architect (TEA) module**: <https://bmad-code-org.github.io/bmad-method-test-architecture-enterprise/>

## Output

Save summary to: `{default_output_file}`

**Done!** Tests generated and verified. Validate against `./checklist.md`.

## On Complete

Run: `python3 {project-root}/_bmad/scripts/resolve_customization.py --skill {skill-root} --key workflow.on_complete`

If the resolved `workflow.on_complete` is non-empty, follow it as the final terminal instruction before exiting.

## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)

5	# Story 3.2: Per-Pipeline Quality Thresholds
6	
7	Status: review
8	
9	## Story
10	
11	As a pipeline operator,
12	I want each pipeline alias to declare its own default quality threshold,
13	So that deepwiki skills are held to 90% while forge skills default to 80%.
14	
15	## Acceptance Criteria
16	
17	1. **Given** a TS invocation within the deepwiki pipeline and no explicit override
18	   **When** TS looks up the threshold
19	   **Then** the default threshold is 90%
20	
21	2. **Given** a TS invocation within the forge pipeline and no explicit override
22	   **When** TS looks up the threshold
23	   **Then** the default threshold is 80%
24	
25	3. **Given** a TS invocation with the `TS[min:85]` override syntax
26	   **When** TS resolves the threshold
27	   **Then** the override value of 85% is used instead of the pipeline default
28	   **And** the source of the threshold (override vs default) is logged
29	
30	4. **Given** any pipeline and any threshold configuration
31	   **When** TS runs
32	   **Then** the hard gate (Story 3.1) is enforced before the soft gate regardless of threshold value
33	
34	## Tasks / Subtasks
35	
36	- [x] Task 1: Add per-pipeline threshold lookup table to init.md (AC: #1, #2, #4)
37	  - [x] 1.1 Add a `pipeline_threshold_defaults` lookup table as a new §1b section after §1 and before §2
38	  - [x] 1.2 Resolve `{pipeline_alias}` from workflow data context (set by forger during pipeline execution)
39	  - [x] 1.3 If `{pipeline_alias}` is present AND found in the lookup table, store `{pipeline_default_threshold}` in workflow context
40	  - [x] 1.4 If `{pipeline_alias}` is absent (standalone TS invocation), `{pipeline_default_threshold}` is unset — score.md falls through to `{defaultThreshold}`
41	
42	- [x] Task 2: Update score.md threshold resolution to include pipeline default (AC: #1, #2, #3)
43	  - [x] 2.1 Update §1 "Resolve the pass threshold" to add pipeline default as a precedence layer
44	  - [x] 2.2 New precedence order: CLI `--threshold=N` > `{pipeline_default_threshold}` (from init.md §1b lookup) > `{defaultThreshold}` (from customize.toml, default 80) > fallback 80
45	  - [x] 2.3 Add threshold source logging: record which layer supplied `effective_threshold` (e.g. "pipeline default (deepwiki: 90)" or "CLI override (85)" or "workflow default (80)")
46	  - [x] 2.4 Store `threshold_source` string in workflow context for use in the score report section
47	
48	- [x] Task 3: Pass `{pipeline_alias}` from forger pipeline data context (AC: #1, #2)
49	  - [x] 3.1 In `src/skf-forger/SKILL.md` Pipeline Mode §4.c, add `{pipeline_alias}` to the data context passed to each workflow in the chain (set to the alias name: `deepwiki`, `forge`, `forge-quick`, `onboard`, `maintain`, or `null` for ad-hoc sequences)
50	  - [x] 3.2 In `src/shared/references/pipeline-contracts.md` Pipeline State section, document `pipeline_alias` as part of the pipeline data context forwarded to each workflow
51	
52	- [x] Task 4: Verify hard gate independence (AC: #4)
53	  - [x] 4.1 Confirm `step-hard-gate.md` chains before `score.md` regardless of threshold — no changes needed to hard gate, just verify the chain `external-validators.md → step-hard-gate.md → score.md` is intact and threshold-agnostic
54	
55	## Dev Notes
56	
57	### Per-Pipeline Threshold Lookup Table
58	
59	The architecture specifies this exact table (architecture.md line 223):
60	
61	```
62	pipeline_threshold_defaults:
63	  deepwiki: 90
64	  forge: 80
65	  forge-quick: 80
66	  campaign: 90
67	```
68	
69	This table lives in `init.md` as a new §1b section. It is the canonical source of truth for pipeline-specific defaults. The alias expansion values in the forger (e.g., `TS[min:90]` in deepwiki) should match this table, but the table in TS is what TS itself references.
70	
71	### Threshold Resolution Precedence (Current → New)
72	
73	**Current** (score.md §1):
74	1. CLI `--threshold=N` → highest priority
75	2. `{defaultThreshold}` workflow-context variable (from customize.toml, default 80) → second
76	3. Fallback `80` → last resort
77	
78	**After this story** (score.md §1):
79	1. CLI `--threshold=N` → highest priority (user explicitly typed `--threshold`)
80	2. `{pipeline_default_threshold}` → pipeline-specific default from init.md §1b lookup table (only set when `{pipeline_alias}` is in workflow context)
81	3. `{defaultThreshold}` workflow-context variable (from customize.toml, default 80) → org-wide default
82	4. Fallback `80` → last resort
83	
84	### How `{pipeline_alias}` Reaches TS
85	
86	The forger orchestrates pipeline execution (src/skf-forger/SKILL.md Pipeline Mode). In step 4.c, it invokes each workflow with `{headless_mode}` = true and resolved arguments. The forger already knows the pipeline alias from step 1 (it parsed and expanded the alias).
87	
88	**Change needed:** the forger must also set `{pipeline_alias}` in the workflow data context for each workflow in the chain. This is a small addition to step 4.c — one new variable alongside `{headless_mode}`.
89	
90	For ad-hoc sequences (user typed `BS CS TS EX` directly, not via alias), `{pipeline_alias}` is `null` — no lookup table entry applies, and score.md falls through to `{defaultThreshold}`.
91	
92	### How `TS[min:N]` Interacts with Pipeline Default
93	
94	The bracket syntax `TS[min:N]` is parsed by the forger in step 1 and passed to TS as `--threshold=N`. Since CLI `--threshold` has highest precedence, it always wins over the pipeline default.
95	
96	This means:
97	- `deepwiki` → expands to `TS[min:90]` → forger passes `--threshold=90` → score.md uses 90 (CLI layer, but matches pipeline default)
98	- `deepwiki` with user override `TS[min:85]` → forger passes `--threshold=85` → score.md uses 85 (CLI override, pipeline default would have been 90)
99	- `forge` → expands to `TS` (no bracket) → no `--threshold` passed → score.md uses pipeline default 80 from lookup table
100	- Standalone `TS` invocation → no pipeline context → score.md uses `{defaultThreshold}` from customize.toml = 80
101	
102	### Threshold Source Logging
103	
104	Score.md §1 should record which layer supplied `effective_threshold`:
105	
106	| Scenario | Log message |
107	|----------|-------------|
108	| CLI/bracket override | `Threshold source: CLI override (85%)` |
109	| Pipeline default | `Threshold source: pipeline default (deepwiki → 90%)` |
110	| Customize.toml default | `Threshold source: workflow default (80%)` |
111	| Fallback | `Threshold source: bundled fallback (80%)` |
112	
113	This log line goes into the score report output and can optionally be added to the output frontmatter for traceability.
114	
115	### Step-Hard-Gate Chain Independence
116	
117	The step chain is (from story 3.1):
118	```
119	... → external-validators.md → step-hard-gate.md → score.md → report.md → ...
120	```
121	
122	The hard gate (`step-hard-gate.md`) scans for critical/high severity findings and blocks if any exist. It does NOT reference the threshold — it's purely severity-based. The threshold only matters in `score.md` (the soft gate). This means AC #4 is already satisfied by the existing chain — the hard gate runs before score.md regardless of which pipeline or threshold is in effect.
123	
124	No changes needed to `step-hard-gate.md`.
125	
126	### What Must NOT Change
127	
128	- `step-hard-gate.md` — no modifications; hard gate is threshold-independent
129	- `scoring-rules.md` — no modifications; scoring rules are pipeline-independent
130	- `coverage-check.md` / `coherence-check.md` — no modifications; analysis is threshold-independent
131	- `customize.toml` `default_threshold = 80` — this remains the org-wide default; pipeline defaults are a layer above it
132	- `compute-score.py` — no modifications; the script already accepts `threshold` as input JSON field
133	- Exit codes — no changes; same codes apply regardless of threshold source
134	
135	### Project Structure Notes
136	
137	- No new files created — only modifications to existing files
138	- `src/skf-test-skill/references/init.md` — gains §1b (per-pipeline threshold lookup table + resolution)
139	- `src/skf-test-skill/references/score.md` — §1 threshold resolution updated with pipeline default layer + source logging
140	- `src/skf-forger/SKILL.md` — Pipeline Mode §4.c gains `{pipeline_alias}` in data context
141	- `src/shared/references/pipeline-contracts.md` — Pipeline State section documents `pipeline_alias` forwarding
142	
143	### Story 3.1 Learnings (Apply These)
144	
145	From the previous story's debug log and retro commitments:
146	
147	1. **Self-audit for internal references**: Do NOT include internal story IDs, epic numbers, or bmad keywords in the step file content or commit messages
148	2. **Cross-reference grep when modifying step files**: After modifying init.md and score.md, grep for any other files that reference threshold resolution or `{defaultThreshold}` to ensure consistency
149	3. **Section numbering convention**: Use §-prefixed sections within step files (§1, §2, etc.) — the new section in init.md should be §1b to slot between existing §1 and §2
150	4. **Backward compatibility**: Verify the new threshold precedence layer does not change behavior for standalone TS invocations (no `{pipeline_alias}` → falls through to `{defaultThreshold}` → same 80% as before)
151	
152	### Cross-Reference Check
153	
154	After modifying init.md and score.md, verify these for consistency:
155	- `src/skf-test-skill/SKILL.md` — Invocation Contract table mentions `--threshold=<N>` (should reference pipeline default in the threshold description)
156	- `src/skf-test-skill/customize.toml` — `default_threshold = 80` comment should note that pipeline defaults (from init.md §1b) take precedence over this value when TS runs in a pipeline
157	- `src/skf-forger/SKILL.md` — deepwiki alias expansion `TS[min:90]` should match the lookup table's deepwiki entry
158	- `src/shared/references/pipeline-contracts.md` — Circuit Breakers table default threshold for TS should note per-pipeline defaults exist
159	
160	### References
161	
162	- [Source: _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/epics.md — Story 3.2 "Per-pipeline quality thresholds"]
163	- [Source: _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/architecture.md — "Quality Gate Architecture" section, line 223: per-pipeline defaults lookup table]
164	- [Source: src/skf-test-skill/SKILL.md — Invocation Contract, Exit Codes, Result Contract]
165	- [Source: src/skf-test-skill/references/init.md — §1 Receive Skill Path, §4 Load Forge Tier]
166	- [Source: src/skf-test-skill/references/score.md — §1 Load Scoring Rules, threshold resolution precedence]
167	- [Source: src/skf-test-skill/references/step-hard-gate.md — threshold-independent hard gate chain]
168	- [Source: src/skf-test-skill/customize.toml — default_threshold = 80]
169	- [Source: src/skf-forger/SKILL.md — Pipeline Mode §1 alias parsing, §4.c workflow invocation]
170	- [Source: src/shared/references/pipeline-contracts.md — Data Flow, Circuit Breakers, Bracket Syntax]
171	- [Source: _bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/3-1-tiered-quality-gate-hard-gate.md — Story 3.1 learnings]
172	
173	## Dev Agent Record
174	
175	### Agent Model Used
176	
177	Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context)
178	
179	### Debug Log References

181	- Verified step-hard-gate.md contains zero threshold references (only `threshold: null` in headless JSON envelope — not used for gate logic)
182	- Verified step chain: external-validators.md → step-hard-gate.md → score.md via nextStepFile frontmatter
183	- Cross-reference check completed: updated SKILL.md Invocation Contract, customize.toml comment, init.md --threshold flag description, and pipeline-contracts.md Circuit Breakers table
184	- Full test suite passed: 0 errors across schema, install, CLI, workflow, Python, knowledge, validation, lint, and format checks
185	
186	### Completion Notes List
187

189	- Task 2: Updated score.md §1 threshold resolution to 4-layer precedence (CLI > pipeline default > workflow scalar > bundled fallback). Added `threshold_source` logging to workflow context, score report output (new `**Threshold Source:**` line), and output frontmatter (`thresholdSource` field).
190	- Task 3: Updated forger SKILL.md Pipeline Mode §4.c to forward `{pipeline_alias}` to each workflow. Updated pipeline-contracts.md Pipeline State to document `pipeline_alias` in both `pipeline.alias` and `pipeline.data.pipeline_alias`.
191	- Task 4: Verified hard gate independence — step-hard-gate.md is severity-based only, has no threshold references, and chains before score.md regardless of pipeline or threshold configuration.
192	- Cross-reference updates: SKILL.md Invocation Contract, customize.toml precedence comment, init.md --threshold description, SKILL.md On Activation §3, pipeline-contracts.md Circuit Breakers table.

# Story 3.2: Per-Pipeline Quality Thresholds

Status: review

## Story

As a pipeline operator,
I want each pipeline alias to declare its own default quality threshold,
So that deepwiki skills are held to 90% while forge skills default to 80%.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. **Given** a TS invocation within the deepwiki pipeline and no explicit override
   **When** TS looks up the threshold
   **Then** the default threshold is 90%

2. **Given** a TS invocation within the forge pipeline and no explicit override
   **When** TS looks up the threshold
   **Then** the default threshold is 80%

3. **Given** a TS invocation with the `TS[min:85]` override syntax
   **When** TS resolves the threshold
   **Then** the override value of 85% is used instead of the pipeline default
   **And** the source of the threshold (override vs default) is logged

4. **Given** any pipeline and any threshold configuration
   **When** TS runs
   **Then** the hard gate (Story 3.1) is enforced before the soft gate regardless of threshold value

## Tasks / Subtasks

- [x] Task 1: Add per-pipeline threshold lookup table to init.md (AC: #1, #2, #4)
  - [x] 1.1 Add a `pipeline_threshold_defaults` lookup table as a new §1b section after §1 and before §2
  - [x] 1.2 Resolve `{pipeline_alias}` from workflow data context (set by forger during pipeline execution)
  - [x] 1.3 If `{pipeline_alias}` is present AND found in the lookup table, store `{pipeline_default_threshold}` in workflow context
  - [x] 1.4 If `{pipeline_alias}` is absent (standalone TS invocation), `{pipeline_default_threshold}` is unset — score.md falls through to `{defaultThreshold}`

- [x] Task 2: Update score.md threshold resolution to include pipeline default (AC: #1, #2, #3)
  - [x] 2.1 Update §1 "Resolve the pass threshold" to add pipeline default as a precedence layer
  - [x] 2.2 New precedence order: CLI `--threshold=N` > `{pipeline_default_threshold}` (from init.md §1b lookup) > `{defaultThreshold}` (from customize.toml, default 80) > fallback 80
  - [x] 2.3 Add threshold source logging: record which layer supplied `effective_threshold` (e.g. "pipeline default (deepwiki: 90)" or "CLI override (85)" or "workflow default (80)")
  - [x] 2.4 Store `threshold_source` string in workflow context for use in the score report section

- [x] Task 3: Pass `{pipeline_alias}` from forger pipeline data context (AC: #1, #2)
  - [x] 3.1 In `src/skf-forger/SKILL.md` Pipeline Mode §4.c, add `{pipeline_alias}` to the data context passed to each workflow in the chain (set to the alias name: `deepwiki`, `forge`, `forge-quick`, `onboard`, `maintain`, or `null` for ad-hoc sequences)
  - [x] 3.2 In `src/shared/references/pipeline-contracts.md` Pipeline State section, document `pipeline_alias` as part of the pipeline data context forwarded to each workflow

- [x] Task 4: Verify hard gate independence (AC: #4)
  - [x] 4.1 Confirm `step-hard-gate.md` chains before `score.md` regardless of threshold — no changes needed to hard gate, just verify the chain `external-validators.md → step-hard-gate.md → score.md` is intact and threshold-agnostic

## Dev Notes

### Per-Pipeline Threshold Lookup Table

The architecture specifies this exact table (architecture.md line 223):

```
pipeline_threshold_defaults:
  deepwiki: 90
  forge: 80
  forge-quick: 80
  campaign: 90
```

This table lives in `init.md` as a new §1b section. It is the canonical source of truth for pipeline-specific defaults. The alias expansion values in the forger (e.g., `TS[min:90]` in deepwiki) should match this table, but the table in TS is what TS itself references.

### Threshold Resolution Precedence (Current → New)

**Current** (score.md §1):
1. CLI `--threshold=N` → highest priority
2. `{defaultThreshold}` workflow-context variable (from customize.toml, default 80) → second
3. Fallback `80` → last resort

**After this story** (score.md §1):
1. CLI `--threshold=N` → highest priority (user explicitly typed `--threshold`)
2. `{pipeline_default_threshold}` → pipeline-specific default from init.md §1b lookup table (only set when `{pipeline_alias}` is in workflow context)
3. `{defaultThreshold}` workflow-context variable (from customize.toml, default 80) → org-wide default
4. Fallback `80` → last resort

### How `{pipeline_alias}` Reaches TS

The forger orchestrates pipeline execution (src/skf-forger/SKILL.md Pipeline Mode). In step 4.c, it invokes each workflow with `{headless_mode}` = true and resolved arguments. The forger already knows the pipeline alias from step 1 (it parsed and expanded the alias).

**Change needed:** the forger must also set `{pipeline_alias}` in the workflow data context for each workflow in the chain. This is a small addition to step 4.c — one new variable alongside `{headless_mode}`.

For ad-hoc sequences (user typed `BS CS TS EX` directly, not via alias), `{pipeline_alias}` is `null` — no lookup table entry applies, and score.md falls through to `{defaultThreshold}`.

### How `TS[min:N]` Interacts with Pipeline Default

The bracket syntax `TS[min:N]` is parsed by the forger in step 1 and passed to TS as `--threshold=N`. Since CLI `--threshold` has highest precedence, it always wins over the pipeline default.

This means:
- `deepwiki` → expands to `TS[min:90]` → forger passes `--threshold=90` → score.md uses 90 (CLI layer, but matches pipeline default)
- `deepwiki` with user override `TS[min:85]` → forger passes `--threshold=85` → score.md uses 85 (CLI override, pipeline default would have been 90)
- `forge` → expands to `TS` (no bracket) → no `--threshold` passed → score.md uses pipeline default 80 from lookup table
- Standalone `TS` invocation → no pipeline context → score.md uses `{defaultThreshold}` from customize.toml = 80

### Threshold Source Logging

Score.md §1 should record which layer supplied `effective_threshold`:

| Scenario | Log message |
|----------|-------------|
| CLI/bracket override | `Threshold source: CLI override (85%)` |
| Pipeline default | `Threshold source: pipeline default (deepwiki → 90%)` |
| Customize.toml default | `Threshold source: workflow default (80%)` |
| Fallback | `Threshold source: bundled fallback (80%)` |

This log line goes into the score report output and can optionally be added to the output frontmatter for traceability.

### Step-Hard-Gate Chain Independence

The step chain is (from story 3.1):
```
... → external-validators.md → step-hard-gate.md → score.md → report.md → ...
```

The hard gate (`step-hard-gate.md`) scans for critical/high severity findings and blocks if any exist. It does NOT reference the threshold — it's purely severity-based. The threshold only matters in `score.md` (the soft gate). This means AC #4 is already satisfied by the existing chain — the hard gate runs before score.md regardless of which pipeline or threshold is in effect.

No changes needed to `step-hard-gate.md`.

### What Must NOT Change

- `step-hard-gate.md` — no modifications; hard gate is threshold-independent
- `scoring-rules.md` — no modifications; scoring rules are pipeline-independent
- `coverage-check.md` / `coherence-check.md` — no modifications; analysis is threshold-independent
- `customize.toml` `default_threshold = 80` — this remains the org-wide default; pipeline defaults are a layer above it
- `compute-score.py` — no modifications; the script already accepts `threshold` as input JSON field
- Exit codes — no changes; same codes apply regardless of threshold source

### Project Structure Notes

- No new files created — only modifications to existing files
- `src/skf-test-skill/references/init.md` — gains §1b (per-pipeline threshold lookup table + resolution)
- `src/skf-test-skill/references/score.md` — §1 threshold resolution updated with pipeline default layer + source logging
- `src/skf-forger/SKILL.md` — Pipeline Mode §4.c gains `{pipeline_alias}` in data context
- `src/shared/references/pipeline-contracts.md` — Pipeline State section documents `pipeline_alias` forwarding

### Story 3.1 Learnings (Apply These)

From the previous story's debug log and retro commitments:

1. **Self-audit for internal references**: Do NOT include internal story IDs, epic numbers, or bmad keywords in the step file content or commit messages
2. **Cross-reference grep when modifying step files**: After modifying init.md and score.md, grep for any other files that reference threshold resolution or `{defaultThreshold}` to ensure consistency
3. **Section numbering convention**: Use §-prefixed sections within step files (§1, §2, etc.) — the new section in init.md should be §1b to slot between existing §1 and §2
4. **Backward compatibility**: Verify the new threshold precedence layer does not change behavior for standalone TS invocations (no `{pipeline_alias}` → falls through to `{defaultThreshold}` → same 80% as before)

content.text: Now let me understand the testing patterns and the actual changed files to identify what needs testing.

content.content:
1	"""Structural integration tests for the step-hard-gate.md step file (story 3.1).
2	
3	Validates the hard gate step contract: correct pipeline wiring, required
4	sections, severity-scanning behaviour, blocking/pass-through paths, headless
5	envelope emission, stages-table positioning, exit code documentation, and
6	stepsCompleted integration in report.md.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import pathlib
12	import re
13	
14	import pytest
15	
16	REPO_ROOT = pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
17	TS_DIR = REPO_ROOT / "src" / "skf-test-skill"
18	STEP_FILE = TS_DIR / "references" / "step-hard-gate.md"
19	EXT_VALIDATORS_FILE = TS_DIR / "references" / "external-validators.md"
20	SCORE_FILE = TS_DIR / "references" / "score.md"
21	SKILL_MD = TS_DIR / "SKILL.md"
22	TEMPLATE_FILE = TS_DIR / "templates" / "test-report-template.md"
23	REPORT_FILE = TS_DIR / "references" / "report.md"
24	
25	
26	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
27	# Helpers
28	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
29	
30	
31	def _read(path: pathlib.Path) -> str:
32	    return path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
33	
34	
35	def _frontmatter(path: pathlib.Path) -> str | None:
36	    text = _read(path)
37	    m = re.match(r"^---\n(.*?)\n---\n", text, re.DOTALL)
38	    return m.group(1) if m else None
39	
40	
41	def _next_step_value(path: pathlib.Path) -> str | None:
42	    fm = _frontmatter(path)
43	    if fm is None:
44	        return None
45	    m = re.search(r"^nextStepFile:\s*['\"]?([^'\"\n]+?)['\"]?\s*$", fm, re.MULTILINE)
46	    return m.group(1).strip() if m else None
47	
48	
49	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
50	# Step file existence
51	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
52	
53	
54	def test_step_file_exists() -> None:
55	    assert STEP_FILE.exists(), "step-hard-gate.md must exist"
56	
57	
58	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
59	# Pipeline chain values
60	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
61	
62	
63	class TestPipelineChain:
64	    def test_external_validators_points_to_step_hard_gate(self) -> None:
65	        assert _next_step_value(EXT_VALIDATORS_FILE) == "step-hard-gate.md"
66	
67	    def test_step_hard_gate_points_to_score(self) -> None:
68	        assert _next_step_value(STEP_FILE) == "score.md"
69	
70	    def test_score_file_exists(self) -> None:
71	        target = (STEP_FILE.parent / "score.md").resolve()
72	        assert target.exists(), "score.md must exist for the chain to complete"
73	
74	
75	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
76	# Step file frontmatter
77	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
78	
79	
80	class TestStepFileFrontmatter:
81	    @pytest.fixture(scope="class")
82	    def fm(self) -> str:
83	        result = _frontmatter(STEP_FILE)
84	        assert result is not None, "step-hard-gate.md must have frontmatter"
85	        return result
86	
87	    def test_has_next_step_file(self, fm: str) -> None:
88	        assert "nextStepFile" in fm
89	
90	    def test_has_output_file(self, fm: str) -> None:
91	        assert "outputFile" in fm
92	
93	    def test_output_file_pattern(self, fm: str) -> None:
94	        assert re.search(r"outputFile:.*\{forge_version\}", fm), (
95	            "outputFile must use {forge_version} placeholder"

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Contract tests for compute-score.py.
3	
4	Validates that the Python implementation produces identical output for all
5	test fixtures. Fixtures were originally captured from the JavaScript
6	implementation and serve as the source of truth for Python parity.
7	"""
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import json
11	import importlib.util
12	from pathlib import Path
13	
14	import pytest
15	
16	spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(
17	    "compute_score",
18	    Path(__file__).parent.parent / "src" / "skf-test-skill" / "scripts" / "compute-score.py",
19	)
20	mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
21	spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
22	compute_score = mod.compute_score
23	
24	FIXTURES_PATH = Path(__file__).parent / "fixtures" / "compute-score-contract.json"
25	
26	
27	def normalize_for_comparison(obj):
28	    """Normalize JSON-serializable object for comparison.
29	
30	    Handles float/int equivalence (e.g., 0 vs 0.0) by round-tripping
31	    through JSON serialization -- the same format both implementations
32	    use for CLI output.
33	    """
34	    return json.loads(json.dumps(obj))
35	
36	
37	def deep_diff(expected, actual, path=""):
38	    """Find all differences between two nested structures."""
39	    diffs = []
40	
41	    if type(expected) != type(actual):
42	        # Allow int/float equivalence
43	        if isinstance(expected, (int, float)) and isinstance(actual, (int, float)):
44	            if expected != actual:
45	                diffs.append(f"{path}: {expected!r} != {actual!r}")
46	        else:
47	            diffs.append(f"{path}: type {type(expected).__name__} != {type(actual).__name__}")
48	        return diffs
49	
50	    if isinstance(expected, dict):
51	        all_keys = set(expected.keys()) | set(actual.keys())
52	        for key in sorted(all_keys):
53	            if key not in expected:
54	                diffs.append(f"{path}.{key}: missing in expected, present in actual = {actual[key]!r}")
55	            elif key not in actual:
56	                diffs.append(f"{path}.{key}: present in expected = {expected[key]!r}, missing in actual")
57	            else:
58	                diffs.extend(deep_diff(expected[key], actual[key], f"{path}.{key}"))
59	    elif isinstance(expected, list):
60	        if len(expected) != len(actual):
61	            diffs.append(f"{path}: list length {len(expected)} != {len(actual)}")
62	        for i in range(min(len(expected), len(actual))):
63	            diffs.extend(deep_diff(expected[i], actual[i], f"{path}[{i}]"))
64	    elif expected != actual:
65	        diffs.append(f"{path}: {expected!r} != {actual!r}")
66	
67	    return diffs
68	
69	
70	def _load_fixtures():
71	    with open(FIXTURES_PATH, encoding="utf-8") as f:
72	        fixtures = json.load(f)
73	    return fixtures
74	
75	
76	def _fixture_ids():
77	    return [fixture["name"] for fixture in _load_fixtures()]
78	
79	
80	def _fixture_params():
81	    return _load_fixtures()
82	
83	
84	class TestComputeScoreContract:
85	    @pytest.mark.parametrize("fixture", _fixture_params(), ids=_fixture_ids())
86	    def test_output_matches_expected(self, fixture):
87	        expected = normalize_for_comparison(fixture["expected_output"])
88	        actual = normalize_for_comparison(compute_score(fixture["input"]))
89	        diffs = deep_diff(expected, actual)
90	        assert not diffs, (
91	            f"Python output differs from JS for '{fixture['name']}':\n"
92	            + "\n".join(diffs[:10])
93	            + (f"\n... and {len(diffs) - 10} more differences" if len(diffs) > 10 else "")
94	        )
95	
96	
97	class TestReferenceAppSkip:
98	    """Behavioral tests for the `referenceApp` skip flag.
99	
100	    A reference-app skill documents wiring patterns, not a library export
101	    surface, so Signature Accuracy + Type Coverage are skipped — the same
102	    redistribution path as a stack skill, with a distinct skip reason. See
103	    scoring-rules.md "Reference-App Skills" and compute-score.py skip set.
104	    """
105	
106	    # Mirror of the `suite_u_stack_skill_deep` fixture so the equivalence-class
107	    # comparison below is anchored on already-validated stack-skill math.
108	    BASE_INPUT = {
109	        "mode": "contextual",
110	        "tier": "Deep",
111	        "scores": {
112	            "exportCoverage": 90,
113	            "signatureAccuracy": None,
114	            "typeCoverage": None,
115	            "coherence": 85,
116	            "externalValidation": 80,
117	        },
118	    }
119	
120	    def test_reference_app_skips_signature_and_type(self):
121	        out = compute_score({**self.BASE_INPUT, "referenceApp": True})
122	        assert set(out["skippedCategories"]) >= {"signatureAccuracy", "typeCoverage"}
123	        assert out["weights"]["signatureAccuracy"] == 0
124	        assert out["weights"]["typeCoverage"] == 0
125	        reason = "reference-app (no library export signatures)"
126	        assert out["skipReasons"]["signatureAccuracy"] == reason
127	        assert out["skipReasons"]["typeCoverage"] == reason
128	
129	    def test_reference_app_redistribution_matches_stack_skill(self):
130	        """`referenceApp` and `stackSkill` share one redistribution equivalence
131	        class: identical weights/scores/result; only the skipReasons differ."""
132	        ref = compute_score({**self.BASE_INPUT, "referenceApp": True})
133	        stack = compute_score({**self.BASE_INPUT, "stackSkill": True})
134	        for key in (
135	            "weights", "weightedScores", "totalScore", "result", "weightSum",
136	            "activeCategories", "skippedCategories",
137	        ):
138	            assert ref[key] == stack[key], f"{key} diverged from stack-skill baseline"
139	        assert ref["skipReasons"]["signatureAccuracy"] == "reference-app (no library export signatures)"
140	        assert stack["skipReasons"]["signatureAccuracy"] == "stack skill (external type surface)"
141	
142	    def test_absent_reference_app_keeps_sig_type_active(self):
143	        """Omitting the flag must not silently skip — proves the change is additive."""
144	        inp = {
145	            "mode": "contextual",
146	            "tier": "Deep",
147	            "scores": {
148	                "exportCoverage": 90,
149	                "signatureAccuracy": 88,
150	                "typeCoverage": 85,
151	                "coherence": 85,
152	                "externalValidation": 80,
153	            },
154	        }
155	        out = compute_score(inp)
156	        assert "signatureAccuracy" not in out["skippedCategories"]
157	        assert "typeCoverage" not in out["skippedCategories"]
158	        assert out.get("skipReasons", {}).get("signatureAccuracy") is None
